In Monograph, as an example, we have the article generation pipeline.

This is the standard pipeline that many of you are still using and working with and are familiar with, where it goes through the kind of processes in these workflows and pulls your article forward through the various workflows, essentially sequentially.
One of the major things that I wanted to talk about, about the old pipelines, is that they work through a process called segmentation.

So they actually split the outline into sections, then they store the research done by the researcher in a Redis database, and then they perform the drafting task on each section, and then assemble it back later on.
So you can see this by looking at, looking at, looking at,
The prompts for this, so you can see that this article draft process is, hey, I have an article that I split into sections and gave to a different writer.

You're an editor-in-chief, you offer, this is, I think, not correct, but that's okay, we're moving on from these pipelines anyway, so I never got a chance to fix that, but the disjointed list of suggestions, I now need you to review this, make sure it's cohesive and easy to read.

And so, you can see what it's trying to do here, it's trying to make sure that there are transitions, remove redundant information that's already in other sections, identify and remove duplicate hyperlinks, keyword stuffing, you know, yada, yada, yada, punctuation, et cetera, right?
But this process of splitting the article into sections, working on each section, and then assembling it, I'm sure all of you have seen, at some point, this process does introduce oddities.
And it can be repetition.
It can be, you know, really hard left turns in transitions between one section and another.
You're like, wait a minute.
What are we talking about now?

And like that, it's just not, this was a byproduct of a couple of things, and I won't get too laborious about it, but the most important thing that it was a byproduct of is that the token windows were so much smaller August of last year.
You literally could not fit enough instructions into a command, into an instruction set to, an operation to enable you to work on the whole article at once.
So that is why the segmentation approach was necessary, but it is now outdated because we do have the ability to have a much larger token window.
So that is where the agentic pipelines are coming from.
They fix that problem by working on essentially the whole article at once.
They also do another thing in that they
They have validation built in.

So one of the things that I'm sure that you have all seen is that you can give it really well-written, precise commands, and it just ignores them, which is only natural, because this is the way LLMs work.
They prioritize, they act on things, and sometimes they lose the plot.
So there is a very important part of the agentic pipeline that I will point out here, and that is the post-processing workflow.
So in the validation step, the post-processing step, it basically says, hey, I could see the writing guidelines that you've given me.
I'm going to make some changes, and then I'm going to run a confidence scoring on those changes.
So you can see here, here's an evaluation report, right?
This article has good tone.
I see some critical issues in Monograph's instruction.
We have, of course, that they want MoneyGantt to have a trademark.
We want an SCTA in a very specific format.
And then we also want an FAQ that's saying, hey, you said you want these and they don't exist in this initial draft.
And then some high priority issues, EM dashes, long hyperlink text, dense text sections, things like that.
These are all born of our process to understand how the articles were coming out of the pipeline versus how we want to deliver them to Monograph, right?
So it runs this evaluation report and then goes back and fixes those changes, fixes those issues.
You can see here it fixed all that stuff.
Then it runs a confidence scoring, an evaluation on that.
And it says, hey, is this confidence score higher than 0.8 or basically is it 80% good?
And then if it's not, it runs it again.
And if it's not, it runs it again.
And if it's not, it runs it again.
it's not
So basically, it will run through, do a correction pass, do an eval, then make the correction pass again, then run an eval, et cetera, until it achieves a high confidence score.
And that's how you get an output that is adherent to the desires that you have.
